host-2015-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'New Scotland Yard' paragraph reports the building emits 13,491 tonnes of CO2 a year — 'the most polluting police station in England and Wales' — so the implication is that they need to rethink their power use. That is what B says.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt is 'What is implied here?' — so the answer is NOT something the text says outright, but the conclusion the author is steering you towards. On 'implied' items, read the whole paragraph and ask: what action or judgement is the writer nudging me towards without stating it?

steps.title: Locate the load-bearing sentences
steps.text: Two sentences carry the message. First: 'Staff at the HQ of the Metropolitan Police use so much lighting, heating, cooling and electricity that the tower pumps out 13,491 tonnes of carbon dioxide a year — equivalent to about 2,200 households.' Then: 'It makes it the most polluting police station in England and Wales and one of the biggest contributors to greenhouse gases of any public building in Britain.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: New Scotland Yard uses an enormous amount of electricity and pollutes more than any other police station. The author isn't saying this to be neutral — the framing ('but the sight of its lights burning… has taken on a different meaning') is critical. The unspoken next step is: they ought to do something about it.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Beacon of law and order' = a guiding symbol of justice. 'Pumps out' = emits in large quantities. 'Reassess' (in option B) = look at again with a view to changing. 'Guiding light' (in option C) is a play on 'beacon' — same root metaphor, but used dismissively.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('working hard to cut down on greenhouse gases') is the opposite of what the text implies — the text says they ARE the biggest polluter, not that they're already fixing it. B ('need to reassess their consumption of electricity') captures the unspoken 'they ought to do something' nudge — clean match. C ('have never been a guiding light') misreads the metaphor — the paragraph isn't questioning their moral authority, it's flagging their power bill. D ('have lost the confidence of the British people') invents a public-opinion claim the text never makes.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The paragraph's whole point is the size of the electricity bill and the pollution that follows — the implied call is for the Yard to look at its energy use again.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to assume that because the text is about emissions, the police MUST be doing something about it — environmental coverage usually frames an actor as either villain or hero.
distractors.why_wrong: The text frames the Yard as the villain here — 'the most polluting police station in England and Wales'. There's no sentence about cutting back. Step 3's paraphrase contains no action by the police.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'beacon of law and order' as a setup for a teardown — and the paragraph IS a teardown — so 'have never been a guiding light' feels like the natural punch line.
distractors.why_wrong: The teardown is about ENERGY USE, not moral standing. 'Guiding light' (C) substitutes a moral critique for an environmental one. Step 2's load-bearing sentences are entirely about CO2 emissions, not law-enforcement reputation.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: a critical paragraph about a public institution implies public anger.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says nothing about what the British public thinks — only what the building emits. D imports an idea (lost confidence) that the paragraph never raises. Step 5's option-by-option walk flags this as content not in the passage.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'What is implied here?' items, the answer paraphrases the writer's UNSPOKEN call-to-action — the next sentence the writer would say if pushed. Look for the option that completes the writer's thought, not the option that adds new content.
host-2015-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: It is tempting to read a critical opening line ('beacon of law and order, but…') as setting up a broader attack. The 'but' here is narrow: it only flips the metaphor (lights = symbol of justice → lights = source of pollution). Don't let one rhetorical reversal pull you into other unstated critiques.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The paragraph says 'nutrient-rich runoff from massive melting glaciers may have provided the extra energy needed to fuel this dramatic evolution' — so the spread of animal life was probably helped by melting ice. That is C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what the text says ABOUT evolution — so you want the claim the paragraph makes, not just background colour. With 'What is said about X' questions, find the sentence that contains X and a verb of claiming ('shows', 'suggests', 'may have').

steps.title: Locate the claim sentence
steps.text: The final sentence carries the argument: 'New research shows that nutrient-rich runoff from massive melting glaciers may have provided the extra energy needed to fuel this dramatic evolution.' The earlier sentence frames the puzzle ('scientists have not been able to figure out exactly what sparked this massive diversification'), and the last sentence resolves it.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: animals spread across the planet around 540 million years ago. Scientists didn't know why. New research suggests melting glacier runoff fed extra nutrients into the oceans, which gave evolution the fuel it needed.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Geologic blink of an eye' = a short time on geological timescales (still millions of years). 'Radiate out' = spread in many directions. 'Runoff' = water flowing off land into the sea. 'Sparked' = triggered, set off. 'Diversification' = becoming more varied.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('many animals have early global warming to thank for their existence') is close but slips on the mechanism — the text talks about MELTING ICE feeding nutrients, not 'global warming' generally; and 'have… to thank for their existence' overstates 'may have provided extra energy'. B ('spread of animals… led to global warming') reverses cause and effect — the text says climate change drove evolution, not the other way round. C ('original spread of animal life… probably helped by melting ice') matches the claim sentence exactly, with the right hedge ('probably' for 'may have'). D ('melting ice almost put an end to animal life') is the opposite — the text says melting ice HELPED life, not harmed it.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. It is the only option that names the right mechanism (melting ice → nutrient runoff → energy for evolution) with the right hedge ('probably' / 'may have').

distractors.why_tempting: Melting ice and global warming live in the same family of ideas, so 'early global warming' feels like a fair stand-in for the paragraph's claim.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph names a specific mechanism — glacial runoff carrying nutrients to the oceans — not 'global warming' as a cause. A also overstates 'may have provided' as 'have… to thank for their existence'. Step 4's gloss of 'runoff' rules A out.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the words 'massive melting glaciers' and 'dramatic evolution' and intuit a feedback loop where animal spread itself drove warming.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph's causal arrow goes the other way: melting glaciers → nutrient runoff → energy for evolution. There is no claim that animal spread caused global warming. B reverses the cause and the effect from step 3.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'massive melting glaciers' as a disaster image, D feels intuitive — ice ages and extinctions are linked in popular science.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph treats melting glaciers as a POSITIVE driver of life ('provided the extra energy needed'), not a near-extinction event. D inverts the paragraph's claim. Step 5's option walk catches the sign flip.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'What is said about X' items, find the sentence that pairs X with a verb of claiming ('shows', 'suggests', 'may have') — that sentence is the answer in disguise. Then check that your chosen option preserves both the MECHANISM and the HEDGE (probably / may / suggests).
host-2015-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: Hedge words ('may have', 'probably', 'suggests') are easy to drop when paraphrasing, but distractors often inflate them into certainties or reverse them. Keep the hedge on your radar during the option walk.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The opening paragraph says 'Although a few Africans rose to positions of power in India, some even establishing small kingdoms, most entered the country as slaves.' So some Africans did NOT come as slaves — exactly what B says.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is TRUE according to the OPENING paragraph specifically. So you are limited to the first paragraph — don't pull evidence from later in the article — and you want the option that is directly supported by that paragraph.

steps.title: Locate the key sentence
steps.text: The crucial sentence is in the opening paragraph: 'Although a few Africans rose to positions of power in India, some even establishing small kingdoms, most entered the country as slaves.' Two halves: a few rose to power (some even founded kingdoms); most arrived as slaves.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Most Africans came to India as slaves. But not all — a minority rose to positions of power, and some even started their own small kingdoms. So 'most' implies 'not all', which means the option that says 'not all African slaves' should be a direct paraphrase.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Rose to positions of power' = became powerful, climbed the social ladder. 'Establishing small kingdoms' = founding their own small states. 'Most entered as slaves' implies the rest entered as something else (free people, soldiers, traders).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option against the opening paragraph only. A ('more Africans sold as slaves to India than to the West Indies') compares NUMBERS across destinations — the opening paragraph doesn't compare totals. B ('Not all Africans in India were imported as slaves') matches the 'although a few… most…' construction exactly — a few did NOT come as slaves. C ('hardly any traces left') is contradicted by 'Their descendants are found today in Gujarat and along the southern coast'. D ('Most slaves in India were of African origin') flips the claim — the paragraph says most AFRICANS came as slaves, not that most SLAVES were African.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Although a few Africans rose to positions of power' is the same as saying 'not all Africans in India were imported as slaves' — the exceptions in the opening paragraph are exactly what B names.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to compare the two destinations the article opens with — Africa-to-West-Indies vs. Africa-to-India — and assume the article is making a numerical point.
distractors.why_wrong: The opening paragraph contrasts FOCUS ('focus was naturally on the shipment of African slaves to the West Indies, but no mention was made…'), not numbers. There is no count claim — the article never says 'more' went one way than the other. Step 5's option walk catches the missing comparison.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'little is known about those slaves shipped in through Bombay and Karachi' and infer that the African presence has faded.
distractors.why_wrong: The very next sentence in the opening paragraph contradicts C directly: 'Their descendants are found today in Gujarat and along the southern coast.' Traces do remain — they just aren't well documented. Step 2's key sentence pairs with this immediate contradiction.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read quickly, 'most entered the country as slaves' can flip into 'most slaves were Africans' — both contain 'most slaves'.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph's subject is AFRICANS (what fraction came as slaves), not SLAVES (what fraction were African). D switches the population being quantified. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the subject pinned on Africans.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'opening paragraph' items, the answer almost always paraphrases a single CONCESSIVE sentence — one with 'Although', 'However', 'while', or 'most… but a few…'. The concession names the exception, and the correct option usually states that exception in plain language.
host-2015-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: Watch the direction of quantifiers: 'most Africans were slaves' and 'most slaves were African' look almost identical but reverse the subject. When the option flips the population being measured, it is wrong even if the words match.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The text says 'the inhabitants preferred to be ruled by their own king, whatever his faults, rather than by the British' — so the people of Oudh refused to accept British rule. That is D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about the kingdom of Oudh — so you scan the Oudh-related sentences for a claim that is not stated outright but follows naturally from what IS stated.

steps.title: Locate the load-bearing sentence
steps.text: The key sentence is in the second paragraph: 'Although there was much wrong in the kingdom, presided over by its last monarch the Nawab Wajid Ali Shah (1822–87), the inhabitants preferred to be ruled by their own king, whatever his faults, rather than by the British.' Reinforced by the next: 'This was something that Lord Dalhousie… had signally failed to appreciate when he ordered Oudh to be annexed.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: Oudh's own ruler had real faults. But the people still preferred him over the British. They didn't want British rule, even though the British framed their own takeover as fixing 'misrule'. So the implied claim: the people of Oudh refused to accept British rule.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Annexed' = formally taken over by another state. 'Misrule' = bad government. 'Princely state' = a semi-independent kingdom under British India. 'Subjected to' (option D) = forced to submit to.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('ruled by the British military for ages') is contradicted by 'the last princely state to be annexed' — Oudh was only just being taken over, not long-occupied. B ('king started the uprising') reverses agency — the British annexation in 1856 came first, the mutiny in 1857–58 followed; the king didn't start it. C ('notorious among the British for its slave trade') is supported nowhere in the Oudh sentences — slave-trading was for buying African servants, not what made Oudh stand out to the British. D ('Its people refused to be subjected to British rule') is the direct implication of 'preferred to be ruled by their own king… rather than by the British'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'Preferred to be ruled by their own king rather than by the British' is the same as 'refused to be subjected to British rule' — D names the implication directly.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to assume colonial India = long British military rule. So 'ruled by the British military for ages' feels like the default background.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly calls Oudh 'the last princely state to be annexed' — it was independent of direct British rule until 1856. Step 2's load-bearing sentence dates the annexation precisely; A contradicts that timeline.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'the Mutiny' and intuit that the local king must have started the rebellion — kings often lead uprisings.
distractors.why_wrong: The sequence in the text is: British annexed Oudh in 1856 (action); mutiny followed in 1857–58 (reaction). The king didn't start the uprising; he was deposed before it. B inverts the causal order in step 3's paraphrase.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: the article IS about Africans imported as slaves, so 'notorious for slave trade' might feel like a fit for Oudh.
distractors.why_wrong: Slave-importing was a private matter of the Nawab and earlier rulers — the British framing of Oudh's annexation was about 'misrule', not slavery (Britain had banned slavery itself). Step 5 flags that the Oudh sentences contain no British-side reaction to its slave trade.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-034.technique: On 'implied about X' items, look for a concessive sentence ('Although X had Y, the people still Z') — the implication usually lives in the second half. The author has already conceded the criticism (Y); what they REALLY want you to take away is the resistance or preference (Z).
host-2015-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: Colonial-history options often layer on plausible-sounding background ('ruled for ages', 'started the uprising', 'notorious for X') that the specific text doesn't support. Stick to the SENTENCES about the entity in question — don't import generic colonial-era knowledge.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: The text says African men and women 'supported their former master, the slave-owning Wajid Ali Shah, rather than the British, who had prohibited slavery' — so former slaves fought FOR their slave owner against the side that had ABOLISHED slavery. That paradox is C.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is PARADOXICAL about the Lucknow fighting. A paradox is something that LOOKS contradictory — you expect one outcome and get the opposite. Scan for a sentence that explicitly flags the surprise.

steps.title: Locate the paradox sentence
steps.text: The flagging sentence is: 'Why these men and women supported their former master, the slave-owning Wajid Ali Shah, rather than the British, who had prohibited slavery, can be answered from British Government records…' The author is literally pointing out the paradox: slaves siding with the slave-owner against the abolitionist side.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: you'd expect former slaves to fight ON THE BRITISH SIDE because the British had banned slavery. Instead, they fought ON THEIR OWNER'S SIDE against the British. That is the paradox — they defended the slave-owner against the abolitionists.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Paradoxical' = seemingly self-contradictory. 'Prohibited slavery' = banned slavery (British had outlawed it in 1833). 'Anti-slavery British' (option C) = same meaning. 'Former master' = the person who used to own them as slaves.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Indians and Africans took part for different reasons') — the text doesn't compare Indian and African motivations; it focuses on why Africans sided with the Nawab. B ('British soldiers did not expect African women to serve') invents a British reaction the passage never reports — the surprise is reader-side, not British-side. C ('Africans defended their slave owner against the anti-slavery British') captures the exact paradox flagged in the text. D ('British were only able to defeat Wajid Ali Shah with the help of his former slaves') reverses the loyalty — the text says the Africans fought FOR the Nawab, not for the British.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. Former slaves fighting to defend their former owner against the side that had banned slavery is the paradox the author is highlighting.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to assume that a mixed force (Indians + Africans) must have had mixed motivations — that feels like a sociological observation the article might make.
distractors.why_wrong: The article is silent on Indian motivations vs. African motivations. It explains why AFRICANS sided with the Nawab; Indians' reasons aren't dissected. Step 5's option walk catches that A leans on content the passage doesn't include.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'African men and women were involved in the thick of the street fighting' and seize on the unusual presence of women fighters as the paradox.
distractors.why_wrong: The text doesn't describe British surprise at female fighters — and the unusual gender mix isn't called a paradox. The flagged surprise (step 2) is about loyalty, not gender. B picks a different feature of the same scene.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember 'street fighting' as a generic battle scene, D's image of Africans helping the British defeat the Nawab can feel plausible — colonial allies often switch sides.
distractors.why_wrong: The text is explicit: the Africans 'supported their former master, the slave-owning Wajid Ali Shah, rather than the British'. D inverts the side they fought for. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the loyalty pinned to the Nawab.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On 'paradox / somewhat paradoxical' items, the author has usually already named the surprise out loud — look for sentences with 'Why X did Y rather than Z' or 'although'. The paradox is in that 'rather than' structure; the correct option will restate both sides of it.
host-2015-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: It is easy to pick a paradox the article HINTS at (like women fighting in 19th-century battles) instead of the one it FLAGS. Trust the sentence that opens with 'Why' or contains a clear 'rather than' — that is the paradox the question is about.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: The text says 'There are fragmentary accounts of Africans in Lucknow brought in by earlier rulers' — including Wajid Ali Shah's great-grandfather and great-uncle. So Africans had been arriving in India well before the 1857 Mutiny. That is B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what we're told about the IMPORT of Africans to India. So the answer is about HOW and WHEN Africans were brought in — scan for sentences that describe the trade itself, not the later mutiny.

steps.title: Locate the supporting sentences
steps.text: Two passages carry the answer. The opening: 'the trade eastwards from Africa to the Indian subcontinent… the Arab traders who sold slaves to wealthy Indian rulers.' And later: 'There are fragmentary accounts of Africans in Lucknow brought in by earlier rulers. Wajid Ali Shah's great-grandfather, a keen horseman, had employed habshi boys as jockeys at the turn of the 19th century. A great-uncle had ordered and bought 18 slaves… Wajid Ali Shah's own father ordered 200 swords for his African troops.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: Africans had been coming to India for generations before the 1857 Mutiny. The great-grandfather already had African jockeys around 1800; the great-uncle bought 18 slaves; the father armed African troops. By the time of Wajid Ali Shah, the practice was decades old.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Mutiny' = the 1857–58 Indian Uprising. 'At the turn of the 19th century' = around 1800. 'Habshi' = a historic term for people of East African origin in India. 'Indian Mutiny' fixes the date anchor: anything 'before' 1857 counts as 'long before the Mutiny'.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('exclusively for military purposes') is too narrow — the text mentions jockeys, slaves, AND troops; not exclusively military. B ('Africans had been brought to India long before the Indian Mutiny') matches the 'earlier rulers… great-grandfather… great-uncle… own father' chain — generations before 1857. C ('common for Indian rulers to use both men and women') is contradicted by 'the Nawabs of Oudh seem unique in importing women as well as men' — Oudh is the EXCEPTION, not the rule. D ('Mutiny was triggered by the Africans') is unsupported — the article never makes the Africans the cause of the Mutiny.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The chain of earlier rulers buying Africans — great-grandfather, great-uncle, father — places the trade well before the 1857 Mutiny.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to focus on the military mentions (200 swords, African troops, fighters in Lucknow) and conclude the trade was mostly about soldiers.
distractors.why_wrong: The text also names habshi BOYS used as jockeys, and 18 slaves bought by a great-uncle — not described as military. 'Exclusively' is too strong. Step 2's chain of uses contradicts A's exclusivity claim.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'Nawabs of Oudh imported women as well as men' and generalise to all Indian rulers.
distractors.why_wrong: The very phrase the option leans on says the OPPOSITE: 'the Nawabs of Oudh seem unique in importing women as well as men'. Oudh is described as the exception, not the rule. Step 5's option walk catches the over-generalisation.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: if the article spends so much time on Africans and the Mutiny, maybe it claims the Africans STARTED the Mutiny.
distractors.why_wrong: The article frames the Mutiny's trigger as the British annexation of Oudh in 1856 — the Indian troops 'who turned against their British officers came from Oudh'. The Africans are participants, not instigators. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the Africans' role as already-present residents, not triggers.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On 'what are we told about the import of X' items, look for the historical depth markers — phrases like 'earlier rulers', 'great-grandfather', 'at the turn of the century'. These set up a 'long before' answer; the correct option usually anchors the practice well before the article's main event.
host-2015-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: Options that say 'exclusively' or 'common for [whole group]' often fail because the text supports a more specific or narrower claim. Watch for the absolute quantifier — and check whether the article actually generalises that far.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The text says the British government DID compensate dependents in Bombay ('many people who had been dependent on the Nawab… did receive small sums from the British government') but DID NOT compensate the Africans in Lucknow ('the Africans' petition was turned down'). Same government, two different outcomes — that is an inconsistent policy. Answer B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks about the British government's ATTITUDE towards former African slaves in India. So the answer is about what the British did or didn't do for them — and how that compares across cases. Scan for sentences describing British action (or inaction) toward the freed Africans.

steps.title: Locate the contrast
steps.text: The article sets up a direct contrast. On one side: 'many people who had been dependent on the Nawab for their livelihood did receive small sums from the British government in compensation for losing their source of income.' On the other: 'the Africans' petition was turned down… The care and financial support given by the British government to slaves in Bombay contrasts starkly with the official indifference shown to the Africans in Lucknow.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: the British gave money to some former dependents of the Nawab but rejected the Africans' petition for pensions in Lucknow. The British looked after slaves in Bombay; they ignored the Africans in Lucknow. Same government, opposite policies in two places. That is inconsistency.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Inconsistent' = applying different rules to similar cases. 'Petition' = a formal request for help. 'Pensions' = regular payments to people no longer earning. 'Indifference' = lack of concern. 'Contrasts starkly with' = is sharply different from.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('usually refused to take responsibility for them') is too one-sided — the text shows British DID take responsibility in Bombay, only refused in Lucknow. B ('policy with regard to economic help to them was inconsistent') captures the Bombay-vs-Lucknow contrast. C ('offered no compensation for their loss of income when freed') is contradicted by the Bombay sentence — compensation WAS offered to former dependents there. D ('recognition of their human rights was ahead of its time') is not supported by anything — the article describes neglect, not progressive rights.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The article literally calls out 'contrasts starkly' between Bombay aid and Lucknow indifference — that is inconsistency in plain language.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the Lucknow rejection ('petition was turned down… died from starvation') and generalise: the British 'usually' refused responsibility.
distractors.why_wrong: The article gives you the COUNTER-example in the same paragraph: Bombay slaves DID get small sums. 'Usually' overstates the pattern. Step 2's twin quotes flatten A's one-sidedness.

distractors.why_tempting: If you remember the Lucknow rejection sentence and stop, 'offered no compensation for their loss of income when freed' feels like a fair summary.
distractors.why_wrong: Bombay slaves were compensated — the article says so explicitly. 'No compensation' is not supported as a blanket claim. Step 3's paraphrase keeps both sides of the contrast visible.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: 19th-century Britain abolished slavery, so one might assume their record on human rights was 'ahead of its time' for the era.
distractors.why_wrong: The article's tone is critical, not laudatory — it focuses on neglect, starvation, and indifference. 'Ahead of its time' contradicts the article's whole framing. Step 5's option walk catches the inverted sentiment.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'attitude towards X' items where the article sets up a Bombay-vs-Lucknow style contrast, the answer is almost always the option that names the CONTRAST itself (inconsistent, mixed, varying) — not the option that names only one side of it.
host-2015-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: When an article gives you two examples in tension, it's tempting to pick whichever one you read most recently. The author has placed the second example deliberately to AMEND the first — the correct option captures both.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The paragraph closes with 'these programmes unearth deeper emotional issues of which the participants were unaware when they originally agreed to take part' — implying participants risk having private issues exposed they didn't sign up for. That is D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about TV psychology. So the answer is the conclusion the text is steering you toward, not a sentence you'll find quoted verbatim. Scan for an ETHICAL or RISK claim — those are the implied takeaways in short feature pieces.

steps.title: Locate the implication sentence
steps.text: The closing sentence is the load-bearing one: 'But ethical questions do arise. Often these programmes unearth deeper emotional issues of which the participants were unaware when they originally agreed to take part.' The author is hinting: people sign up for one thing, then have something deeper and more private dragged out on TV.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: people who go on TV psychology shows may end up with deeper problems exposed than they bargained for. That is a privacy risk — issues they didn't know they had get made public. The author's worry is about participants being exposed beyond what they consented to.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Unearth' = dig up, bring to light. 'Deeper emotional issues' = problems below the surface, often private. 'Sensitive' (in option D) = personal, easily affecting feelings. 'Therapeutic impact' (in option A) = effect that promotes healing.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('no lasting therapeutic impact') is unsupported — the text never claims the therapy effect doesn't last; it talks about ethical risk. B ('viewers dismiss it as a rather boring trend') is contradicted by 'often make for compelling viewing' — viewers find it gripping, not boring. C ('consensus that disadvantages outweigh good effects') is contradicted by 'therapists' views are divided' — there is NO consensus. D ('People taking part run the risk of personally sensitive issues becoming public knowledge') maps directly onto the closing sentence about issues being unearthed without participants' awareness.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The closing 'ethical question' is exactly about private issues being exposed beyond what the participant agreed to — D names that risk in plain language.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'dumbing down and exploitation' and conclude the author thinks TV therapy doesn't really help — and therefore has no lasting effect.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph never makes a claim about whether the therapy works — only about whether it's ETHICAL and how it's portrayed. 'No lasting therapeutic impact' is a different argument. Step 5's option walk separates therapeutic efficacy from ethical concern.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the phrase 'reality TV' and assume the author looks down on it — so dismissive viewer attitude feels in keeping.
distractors.why_wrong: The text says these programmes 'often make for compelling viewing' — the opposite of viewers finding them boring. B inverts the engagement claim. Step 2 shows the viewer-engagement note is positive.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: ethical worries + dumbing down = the bad side wins. 'Disadvantages outweigh good effects' sounds like the natural conclusion.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly says 'therapists' views are divided' — some worry, others are grateful. There is no CONSENSUS that disadvantages win. C overstates the agreement among experts. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the views split.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On 'implied about X' items for short feature pieces, the implication usually lives in the LAST sentence — that's where the author lands the takeaway. If the last sentence raises an ethical or risk question, the answer paraphrases that risk.
host-2015-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: Look for hedging words like 'some worry', 'others are grateful', 'views are divided' earlier in the paragraph — they rule out any 'consensus' option immediately. Distractors love to inflate divided opinion into agreement.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The text says 'Rather than being socialized into the adult speech community, teenagers are resistant… they're reluctant to use your language and they want to retreat into their own dialect.' Compared to 1950 — when the speaker could use parents' language — modern teenagers are MORE eager to speak their own dialect. That is B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks for the MAIN CLAIM about language use in the U.S. So the answer is the headline argument of the paragraph, not a side observation. With 'main claim' items, look for the sentence that names a CHANGE or TREND across time.

steps.title: Locate the main claim
steps.text: The opening sentence frames the trend: 'Since 1915, adults in the U.S. have made huge vocabulary gains and young people only modest ones. Now, that's a symptom of the growing potency of teenage subculture.' The middle sentence drives it home: 'Rather than being socialized into the adult speech community, teenagers are resistant… they're reluctant to use your language and they want to retreat into their own dialect.' And the personal anchor: in 1950 the speaker COULD use his parents' language; teens today resist that.

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: today's teenagers, more than teenagers 50 or 100 years ago, push back against adult speech and prefer their own dialect. The 'teenage subculture' is described as 'modern' — it didn't exist the same way in 1950 or 1915. So eagerness to speak teen-specific language has grown over time.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Socialized into the adult speech community' = learning to speak like grown-ups. 'Resistant' = pushing back. 'Retreat into their own dialect' = withdrawing into their own way of speaking. 'Non-adult language' (in option B) = language not used by adults, i.e. teen slang/dialect.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('In 1950, teenagers found it easier to understand adult language than today') is partly right but reverses the focus — the text says teens today CAN still understand ('They can understand what you say'), but they don't want to USE it. Understanding isn't the issue; willingness is. B ('Today, teenagers are more eager to use non-adult language than 50 or 100 years ago') maps onto 'growing potency of teenage subculture' and the 1950 contrast. C ('Due to more contact with adults, teenagers usually learned more words in 1950 than today') reverses the cause — the text doesn't claim 1950s teens had more contact; it says today's teens RESIST contact. D ('relationship is the same today as in 1915') flatly contradicts the growth claim.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The whole paragraph traces a growing teen resistance to adult language since 1915 — teens today are MORE eager to use their own dialect than teens were 50 or 100 years ago.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to pick A because the paragraph compares 1950 with now — and 'easier to understand' sounds like a natural framing of the shift.
distractors.why_wrong: The text explicitly says today's teens 'CAN understand what you say' — comprehension isn't the issue. The change is in WILLINGNESS TO USE adult language. A swaps the variable. Step 2's middle sentence pins the issue on use, not understanding.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'huge vocabulary gains' for adults and 'modest ones' for young people and intuit a contact-based explanation — more contact, more words.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph doesn't say contact has decreased; it says teens RESIST adopting adult speech. The mechanism is choice, not exposure. Step 3's paraphrase pins the mechanism on resistance, not contact frequency.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: maybe the speaker is reminiscing about the 'good old days' but ultimately concluding things are the same.
distractors.why_wrong: The opening sentence frames a SHIFT since 1915, and the whole paragraph develops it. 'Same today as in 1915' contradicts the main claim. Step 5's option walk catches the direct reversal.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On 'main claim about X' items, the headline is usually the sentence that names a CHANGE across time. Watch for 'Since', 'Now', 'today', 'modern', 'growing' — these flag the trend. The correct option paraphrases that trend in its own terms.
host-2015-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: It is tempting to pick an option that gets the direction right but swaps the VARIABLE being measured (understanding vs. use, contact vs. choice, vocabulary size vs. willingness). Check that the option matches what the paragraph is actually measuring — not just what it's comparing across years.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The study found subjects detected NEGATIVE words better than positive ones in flash exposures, and researchers speculate 'the brain might process negative stimuli faster than positive ones'. That is C — brain activity seems to be QUICKER for negative information.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what we are told in this text. So the answer is the central finding the paragraph reports. With short experiment-based texts, find the finding sentence and the speculative-mechanism sentence — the answer usually paraphrases one of them.

steps.title: Locate the finding
steps.text: Two sentences carry the answer. The finding: 'The subjects were most accurate at detecting the negative words.' The proposed mechanism: 'researchers speculate that the brain might process negative stimuli faster than positive ones.' Plus the alternative: 'A different explanation could be that information processing is equally fast for both types of information but that negative words better capture our attention.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: subjects spotted negative words better than positive ones in a flash test. One proposed reason: the brain works on negative input faster. Another proposed reason: same speed, but negative words pull attention earlier. Either way, the brain handles negative information either faster or with priority.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'A fraction of a second' = a very short time, milliseconds. 'Stimuli' = inputs to the senses. 'Neutral' = neither positive nor negative. 'Speculate' = guess based on evidence — a hedged claim, not a proven one. 'Briefly' (in option D) = for a short time.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Positive information is most easily understood') is the OPPOSITE of the finding — negative words were detected best, not positive ones. B ('brain activity to be similar for negative and neutral information') misses the central comparison — the study compared positive vs. negative, not negative vs. neutral; and similarity isn't the finding. C ('Brain activity seems to be quicker when dealing with negative information') matches the 'brain might process negative stimuli faster' speculation directly. D ('Brain activity seems relatively slow when briefly exposed to single words') over-generalises 'briefly exposed' into a slowness claim the text doesn't make.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The proposed mechanism is exactly 'the brain processes negative stimuli faster' — C states that finding with the right hedge ('seems to be quicker').

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to flip the finding the right way around — people are usually MORE accurate with positive content, so 'positive most easily understood' feels intuitive.
distractors.why_wrong: The study found the OPPOSITE: negative words were detected most accurately. A inverts the finding from step 2. The intuition runs against the data.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'neutral or had emotional content' and assume the article is about distinguishing neutral from other types — making 'similar for negative and neutral' feel like a comparison the study made.
distractors.why_wrong: The contrast in the speculation sentence is between NEGATIVE and POSITIVE, not negative and neutral. And the finding is about a DIFFERENCE (negative wins), not similarity. Step 5's option walk catches the wrong comparison.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: 'a fraction of a second — too short a time to consciously read the word' sounds like a claim that brief exposure SLOWS the brain.
distractors.why_wrong: The brief exposure is the EXPERIMENTAL METHOD, not a finding about brain speed. The text doesn't say the brain is generally slow with brief exposure — only that the brief exposure prevents conscious reading. D mistakes the method for the result.

host-2015-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On short experiment-summary texts, the answer almost always paraphrases the proposed MECHANISM (the 'researchers speculate…' sentence), not the experimental setup. Skip the procedure details; find the proposed explanation.
host-2015-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: Be careful with options that swap the variables compared in the study (negative vs. positive ↔ negative vs. neutral). The study has a specific contrast — the correct option preserves that exact contrast, not a neighbouring one.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: The gap is 'Physicians have ___ vitamin D supplements to their patients for a decade' — and the rationale that follows is positive ('with good reason: dozens of studies have shown a correlation between high intake of vitamin D… and lower rates of chronic diseases'). So physicians were urging patients to take vitamin D — they have RECOMMENDED it. Answer A.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: This is a single-verb cloze: the gap is a past-participle verb that takes 'vitamin D supplements' as the object and 'to their patients' as the indirect object. So you need a verb of the form 'has [V-ed] X to patients' where X = supplements. The structure 'has X-ed Y to Z' narrows the verb to one that takes a 'to-recipient' frame.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: The very next clause says 'with good reason: dozens of studies have shown a correlation between high intake of vitamin D… and lower rates of chronic diseases'. That justifies giving / suggesting vitamin D. The whole article then debates whether this practice was correct.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The gap describes what physicians did with vitamin D supplements vis-à-vis their patients. The next sentence undercuts the practice ('vitamin D supplements were unnecessary for most Americans and potentially harmful'). So the article is reporting on a doctor-to-patient PRESCRIBING or ADVISING action that ten years of evidence supported.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Gloss each option in this exact frame ('Physicians have ___ vitamin D supplements TO their patients'). A 'recommended' = advised, suggested — physicians have ADVISED vitamin D to their patients. Idiomatic in a 'recommend X to Y' construction. B 'committed' = pledged or assigned; you can 'commit yourself to' something, but 'commit supplements to patients' is not idiomatic. C 'attributed' = credited; you 'attribute X to Y' meaning 'say X is caused by Y' — wrong relation here (physicians didn't say supplements were CAUSED BY their patients). D 'forbidden' = prohibited; but the 'with good reason' that follows praises supplements — so banning them doesn't fit.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug in A: 'Physicians have RECOMMENDED vitamin D supplements to their patients for a decade, with good reason…' Reads naturally and the 'with good reason' justification follows on. Plug in B: 'Physicians have COMMITTED vitamin D supplements to their patients' — wrong frame; 'commit to' takes a goal, not a recipient. Plug in C: 'Physicians have ATTRIBUTED vitamin D supplements to their patients' — would mean physicians credited the supplements to the patients (i.e. blamed patients), which is nonsensical. Plug in D: 'Physicians have FORBIDDEN vitamin D supplements to their patients, with good reason' — directly contradicts the next sentence about positive evidence.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is A. Only 'recommended' fits both the 'X to recipient' frame AND the positive 'with good reason' that follows. The whole article is set up as: doctors recommended → new institute report questions it.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Committed' has a sense of 'devoted to / pledged' that can feel close to 'firmly given' — and the doctors HAVE been firmly giving vitamin D for a decade.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Commit' takes the wrong frame: you commit yourself TO something, not commit supplements TO patients. Step 4's gloss check rules out B on grammar; the relationship is direction-of-action, not pledge.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'attribute' as a doctor-y verb and assume it relates to medical practice.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Attribute X to Y' means 'credit X as caused by Y' — that would mean doctors said supplements came from the patients, which is nonsense. Step 5's plug-test exposes the absurdity.

distractors.why_tempting: If you skim and notice the article later questions vitamin D's benefits, 'forbidden' might feel like the verdict you're being led to.
distractors.why_wrong: The very next phrase is 'with good reason: dozens of studies…' — a justification for SUPPORTING supplements, not banning them. D contradicts the immediate clause that follows the gap. Step 2's read-around check catches D's reversal.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-031.technique: On 'verb + to + recipient' cloze gaps, two things narrow the choice: (1) the verb must take a 'to-recipient' frame ('recommend X to Y', 'send X to Y'), (2) the surrounding tone (positive 'with good reason' vs. negative 'unfortunately') fixes the verb's polarity. Use both filters and the gap collapses to one option.
host-2015-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: Verbs like 'attribute' and 'commit' have plausible doctor-flavour but the wrong syntactic frame. Always test 'does the verb GRAMMATICALLY accept X to Y here?' before letting tone push you toward a choice.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: The gap is in 'the Institute of Medicine… concluded… that vitamin D supplements were unnecessary for most Americans and ___ harmful'. The Institute hedged — supplements MIGHT be harmful, not that they ARE. So we need a hedge meaning 'possibly'. That is B ('potentially').

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Single-word adverb cloze modifying 'harmful'. The gap pairs 'unnecessary' + ADV + 'harmful' — two judgements joined by 'and'. The adverb has to fit before 'harmful' and match the hedge level the Institute is using.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full clause: 'when the Institute of Medicine… concluded in November that vitamin D supplements were unnecessary for most Americans and ___ harmful, patients were understandably confused.' The Institute is making an official recommendation; institutes hedge their warnings about possible harm rather than making absolute claims.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The Institute is saying two things about supplements: (a) they aren't needed for most people, and (b) they MAY do harm. The 'may do harm' framing is a HEDGE — institutes don't usually issue 'supplements ARE harmful' verdicts without strong evidence. So the adverb has to soften 'harmful' to 'might be harmful'.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Gloss each option modifying 'harmful'. A 'accordingly' = therefore, in line with that — but there's no preceding cause/reason for the 'accordingly' to refer to in the right way; doesn't soften 'harmful'. B 'potentially' = possibly, might be — softens 'harmful' to 'might be harmful'. Classic hedge for medical warnings. C 'substantially' = to a large degree — INTENSIFIES harm rather than hedging it; an institute wouldn't issue 'substantially harmful' for vitamin D after a decade of recommending it. D 'increasingly' = more and more over time — implies harm is GROWING, which isn't supported by the surrounding text.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug in A: 'unnecessary for most Americans and ACCORDINGLY harmful' — would mean 'and therefore harmful', which doesn't follow logically (unnecessary doesn't entail harmful). Plug in B: 'unnecessary for most Americans and POTENTIALLY harmful' — the Institute hedges its harm claim, fits the cautious tone. Plug in C: 'unnecessary for most Americans and SUBSTANTIALLY harmful' — too strong; the next paragraph debates the evidence, not confirms heavy harm. Plug in D: 'unnecessary for most Americans and INCREASINGLY harmful' — would need evidence of rising harm over time, which the text doesn't provide.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. 'Potentially harmful' is the standard institutional hedge — possibly harmful, not definitely so. The article's 'rift among experts' framing supports a hedged, not absolute, harm claim.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'unnecessary and accordingly harmful' as a clean cause-and-effect chain — unnecessary, therefore problematic.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Accordingly' needs an explicit cause to follow from, and 'unnecessary' doesn't logically lead to 'harmful'. Many unnecessary things are harmless. Step 5's plug-test breaks the logical chain.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'harmful' and imagine the institute issuing a strong warning — 'substantially harmful' sounds like the kind of bold claim that would create confusion.
distractors.why_wrong: The article describes 'a rift among experts' and 'discarded a raft of observational studies' — not a definitive harm finding. An institute that just yesterday endorsed supplements doesn't pivot to 'substantially harmful' in one report. Step 4's gloss flags the intensifier mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: 'increasingly' is a common adverb in health writing — health warnings often describe growing risks.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Increasingly harmful' needs a time-series of evidence ('more and more harmful over the years') — the text doesn't provide that. The Institute report is a single judgement, not a trend claim. Step 5's plug-test highlights the missing time dimension.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-032.technique: On adverb-cloze gaps modifying a sharp word ('harmful', 'effective', 'dangerous'), check the SPEAKER's institutional position. Government / medical institutes almost always HEDGE their claims with words like 'potentially', 'possibly', 'somewhat' — distractors that intensify or add time dynamics usually miss the institutional voice.
host-2015-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: Watch for adverbs that change the LOGICAL relationship (accordingly = therefore) when the surrounding clauses don't support that logic. 'Accordingly' isn't just a softener — it requires a clear cause-and-effect chain that vitamin-D-unnecessary doesn't supply.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: The gap is 'over what constitutes valid proof when it ___ nutrition'. The subject 'it' refers to the rift / debate; the phrase needs to mean 'when the debate is about / concerns nutrition'. 'Comes to' (D) is the idiom — 'when it comes to nutrition' = 'when nutrition is the topic'.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Multi-word verb cloze. The frame is 'when it ___ nutrition'. The pronoun 'it' is the subject and 'nutrition' is the object. The whole clause is a topical introduction — like saying 'on the matter of nutrition' or 'where nutrition is concerned'.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full sentence: 'The issue exposes a rift among experts over what constitutes valid proof when it ___ nutrition and could affect medical advice on many other supplements.' Translation: when the topic is nutrition, experts disagree about what counts as valid proof.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: 'When it ___ X' is a fixed-idiom slot in English: 'when it comes to X' = 'when the topic is X'. This is one of the most common discourse markers in feature writing. The other options either don't make a clean idiom with 'when it' or change the meaning entirely.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Gloss each option in the 'when it ___ nutrition' frame. A 'refers to' = points to / mentions — 'when it refers to nutrition' means 'when it mentions nutrition', possible but very passive and not idiomatic at sentence-opening level. B 'deals with' = handles / addresses — 'when it deals with nutrition' fits a textbook, not a debate ABOUT nutrition. C 'rules out' = excludes — 'when it rules out nutrition' would mean 'when the rift eliminates nutrition', nonsense here. D 'comes to' = is about (idiom 'when it comes to X' = on the topic of X).

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug in A: 'a rift among experts over what constitutes valid proof when it refers to nutrition' — clunky; 'refer to' wants a more active subject (a passage refers to, a study refers to). The 'it' here is too vague for 'refer'. Plug in B: 'when it deals with nutrition' — 'deal with' wants an active agent (a doctor deals with patients); the rift itself doesn't really 'deal with' nutrition. Plug in C: 'when it rules out nutrition' — would mean 'when the rift eliminates nutrition as a topic', which contradicts the sentence (the rift IS about nutrition). Plug in D: 'when it COMES TO nutrition' — perfect idiom, reads cleanly: 'over what counts as valid proof when the topic is nutrition'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. 'When it comes to nutrition' is the standard idiom for 'on the topic of nutrition' — the only option that produces a natural English sentence here.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'refers to' as a generic topic-marker — and 'refer to' does often introduce a topic in academic writing.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Refer to' needs an agent that does the referring (a text refers, a study refers). The dummy subject 'it' before 'refers' produces an awkward, ungrammatical-feeling sentence. Step 5's plug-test shows 'when it refers to' is not idiomatic English.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'deals with' as a plain way of saying 'is about' — and 'deal with' is colloquially used as 'address'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Deal with' takes an active subject — a doctor deals with cases, a chapter deals with the topic. A 'rift' or vague 'it' doesn't 'deal with' something. The idiom feels off. Step 4's gloss flags the agency mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: 'rules out' might fit if 'it' refers to scientific proof — 'when proof rules out nutrition' could mean 'excludes nutrition'.
distractors.why_wrong: The sentence is INTRODUCING nutrition as the topic of debate, not eliminating it. 'Rules out nutrition' contradicts the article's whole point — the rift is ABOUT nutrition. Step 2's read-around exposes the contradiction.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-033.technique: On 'when it ___ X' cloze frames, the answer is overwhelmingly 'comes to' — this is one of the half-dozen most common discourse markers in English. Memorise the frame: 'when it comes to X' = 'on the topic of X'. Distractor verbs (refer / deal / rule) usually need a fuller subject to feel natural.
host-2015-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: Idiomatic English uses 'come to' in places where literal translation breaks down. A non-native reader might reach for 'refers to' as the 'more academic' choice, but the discourse frame demands the idiom — and English idioms don't always feel justified by their parts.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: The gap is 'such epidemiological studies are ___ to clinical studies'. The next clause explains the reasoning: 'because they rely on observations out in the real world, WHERE IT IS IMPOSSIBLE TO CONTROL FOR THE VARIABLES scientists seek to understand.' That uncontrolled-variables framing is a WEAKNESS — so epidemiological studies are weaker than clinical studies. The word for 'lower / weaker than' is INFERIOR. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Adjective cloze: 'X are ___ to Y'. The relationship between epidemiological studies (X) and clinical studies (Y) gets named by the adjective. The 'to' after the gap fixes the construction: 'superior to', 'inferior to', 'equal to' all work; 'distant to' doesn't.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full sentence: 'In theory, such epidemiological studies are ___ to clinical studies because they rely on observations out in the real world, where it is impossible to control for the variables scientists seek to understand.' The 'because' clause is critical — it names the WEAKNESS (no control for variables). And the next sentence: 'Researchers compensate for the lack of control by using large sample sizes…' — compensate FOR what? For inferiority.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: The 'because' clause names a drawback of epidemiological studies vs. clinical studies — no variable control. So the adjective should mean 'worse than' or 'lower in standing than'. 'In theory' also signals that this is the canonical view, even though the article will later challenge it.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Gloss each option in the 'X are ___ to Y' frame. A 'superior' = better than — would mean epidemiological studies are BETTER than clinical ones; but the 'because' clause names a weakness, not a strength. B 'equal' = on the same level — but the 'because' clause names a difference, not a parity. C 'inferior' = worse than, of lower standing — fits the 'cannot control variables' weakness exactly; 'inferior to' is the right preposition. D 'distant' = far away — doesn't make a quality comparison, and 'distant TO' is unidiomatic ('distant FROM' is the natural pairing).

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug in A: 'In theory, epidemiological studies are SUPERIOR to clinical studies because they rely on observations out in the real world, where it is impossible to control for variables' — the 'because' clause is a CRITICISM, so labelling that a reason they are 'superior' is illogical. Plug in B: 'are EQUAL to' — then the 'because' clause needs to support parity, which it doesn't (it names a downside). Plug in C: 'are INFERIOR to' — the 'because' clause justifies the lower standing: no variable control = a real disadvantage. Reads naturally. The follow-up ('compensate for the lack of control') reinforces. Plug in D: 'are DISTANT to' — wrong preposition (it would need 'from') AND no quality comparison.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. The 'because' clause names a weakness (no variable control), so epidemiological studies are ranked LOWER than clinical ones — inferior to.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'observations out in the real world' as a STRENGTH — real-world data feels more authentic — and pick 'superior'.
distractors.why_wrong: The full clause names real-world observation as a problem BECAUSE it means variables can't be controlled. Step 2's 'because' clause is the load-bearing piece — read it carefully and the polarity flips. The next sentence ('compensate for the lack of control') confirms the weakness.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'In theory' and assume the article is staking out a neutral, equal-respect position between two approaches.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'because' clause is a one-sided criticism, not a parity claim. If they were equal, there'd be no reason to name a downside. 'In theory' actually sets up the standard view (which the article will later challenge) — and the standard view is that controlled studies > uncontrolled ones. Step 4's gloss test rules B out.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: 'distant' might evoke the idea of epidemiology being far from controlled lab science.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Distant to' is not idiomatic — English wants 'distant FROM'. And the article isn't measuring distance; it's ranking quality. D fails on both preposition and meaning. Step 5's plug-test catches the preposition clash.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-034.technique: On 'X are ___ to Y' cloze frames, find the 'because' clause that follows — it tells you the DIRECTION of the comparison. Weakness-naming 'because' → INFERIOR; strength-naming 'because' → SUPERIOR. Always check that 'to' is the right preposition for the adjective (superior/inferior/equal take 'to'; distant takes 'from').
host-2015-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: Articles often introduce a position they will later challenge — phrases like 'in theory' signal the SET-UP, not the final verdict. Don't let your awareness of the article's contrarian later turn override the canonical claim in the cloze sentence.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: The gap is 'picking up extra vitamin D from sunlight and food, which can lead to the vitamin's ___'. The argument: people in a control group might accidentally get vitamin D from sunlight and food, which would lead to the vitamin's beneficial effects showing up — confounding the placebo. So the missing word is 'benefits'. Answer C.

steps.title: Understand the gap type
steps.text: Noun cloze. The frame is 'the vitamin's ___'. The vitamin is vitamin D; the noun has to be something the vitamin produces or has, AND something that would CONFOUND a placebo-controlled trial. So we need the vitamin's EFFECTS — the thing the trial is trying to measure.

steps.title: Read around the gap
steps.text: Full sentence: 'true placebo groups are hard to maintain — how do you prevent people in a control group from, say, picking up extra vitamin D from sunlight and food, which can lead to the vitamin's ___?' The placebo group is the one that's supposed to get NO vitamin D — but if they accidentally get some, the trial gets confused. The thing leakage produces is the very effect the researchers are trying to measure: the beneficial outcomes.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.text: Earlier in the article, vitamin D is associated with 'lower rates of chronic diseases, such as cancer and type 1 diabetes' and 'high levels of vitamin D are generally beneficial'. So when extra vitamin D leaks into the placebo group, what shows up are vitamin D's BENEFITS. The placebo group starts behaving like the treatment group — defeating the trial design.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: Gloss each option as 'the vitamin's ___'. A 'mechanisms' = the biological processes by which it works. Real, but mechanisms don't 'leak' from sunlight; mechanisms are inside the body regardless of dose. B 'ingredients' = components — a vitamin has no 'ingredients' beyond itself; vitamins are single compounds, not formulations. C 'benefits' = positive effects — what extra vitamin D produces, and what would confound a placebo trial. D 'drawbacks' = negative effects — but the article ELSEWHERE notes 'high levels of vitamin D are generally beneficial', so the placebo-confound is about benefits not drawbacks.

steps.title: Plug and test
steps.text: Plug in A: 'picking up extra vitamin D from sunlight and food, which can lead to the vitamin's MECHANISMS' — mechanisms aren't a thing you 'lead to'; they're processes that exist whether or not the vitamin is consumed. Plug in B: '… the vitamin's INGREDIENTS' — a vitamin doesn't have ingredients; it IS an ingredient. Plug in C: '… the vitamin's BENEFITS' — natural sense: extra dietary vitamin D would produce the same health benefits the trial wants to attribute only to the supplement. This defeats the placebo. Plug in D: '… the vitamin's DRAWBACKS' — the article's earlier framing ('high levels are generally beneficial') makes 'drawbacks' the wrong polarity for what extra vitamin D produces.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is C. Extra vitamin D from sunlight and food would produce the vitamin's positive health effects in the placebo group, which is exactly what would 'lead to the vitamin's benefits' — and ruin the placebo control.

distractors.why_tempting: 'Mechanisms' sounds scientific and clinical-trial-appropriate — the article is about controlled studies and processes.
distractors.why_wrong: Mechanisms are HOW a vitamin acts inside the body; you don't 'lead to mechanisms' by getting more of the vitamin from sunlight. The placebo-confound issue is about EFFECTS leaking in, not mechanisms being created. Step 4's gloss test isolates the mismatch.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'extra vitamin D from sunlight and food' and think of food as having 'ingredients' — so 'ingredients' associates loosely.
distractors.why_wrong: A vitamin is itself an ingredient of food, not something with 'ingredients'. Step 4's gloss check exposes the category error: vitamins are compounds, not formulations with components.

distractors.why_tempting: If you read fast and remember 'vitamin D supplements were unnecessary for most Americans and potentially harmful', 'drawbacks' might feel consistent with the article's worry.
distractors.why_wrong: The article also says 'high levels of vitamin D are generally beneficial', and the placebo-confound issue is that benefits show up where they shouldn't (in the control group). Drawbacks would be the OPPOSITE confound. Step 3's reading of the surrounding evidence pins the polarity to positive.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-035.technique: On 'leads to the vitamin's ___' cloze gaps about placebo trials, ask: WHAT IS THE TRIAL TRYING TO MEASURE? That is the noun that would confound the trial. Trials measure EFFECTS — so the missing word is almost always 'effects', 'benefits', or 'drawbacks' depending on the polarity earlier in the article.
host-2015-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Don't get pulled by a noun that 'sounds scientific' (mechanisms / ingredients). The cloze rewards meaning, not register. Plug each option in and ask: does extra vitamin D actually CAUSE this noun to appear?

host-2015-verb2-ELF-036.solution_path: The first two paragraphs say 'in some cases it isn't particularly scientific either' and 'expert witnesses have tended to exaggerate what forensic techniques can tell us'. So forensic practitioners overstate what their methods can deliver — they don't seem to realize the limitations. That is B.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks the MAIN FOCUS of the first two paragraphs — so you want the headline criticism, not a side detail. With 'main focus' questions on opinion pieces, the answer usually paraphrases the author's central complaint.

steps.title: Locate the load-bearing sentences
steps.text: Paragraph 1: 'Thanks to TV programmes like CSI… forensics is perceived as a glamorous science. In reality, of course, it is anything but, and in some cases it isn't particularly scientific either… The problem is that expert witnesses have tended to exaggerate what forensic techniques can tell us.' Paragraph 2: 'Forensic science has to transform itself. For a role model it could look to DNA analysis, with its careful attention to what the evidence can and cannot tell us.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: forensics is sold to the public as rigorous science but isn't always — and the people doing it overstate what it can prove. DNA analysis is held up as a contrast: it's careful about its own limits. So the focus is: practitioners overstate; they don't recognise their limits.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Exaggerate' = overstate, claim more than is warranted. 'What forensic techniques can tell us' = the limits of what the methods can prove. 'Limitations' (option B) = the bounds of what a method can show. 'Transform itself' = change fundamentally. 'Glamorous' = attractively exciting (used ironically here).

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('scientific basis of forensics has been greatly extended') is the OPPOSITE of paragraph 1's complaint — the author says forensics is NOT particularly scientific in some cases. B ('Those practising forensic science do not seem to realize its limitations') matches 'expert witnesses have tended to exaggerate what forensic techniques can tell us'. C ('forensic evidence is often played down') is the OPPOSITE — the criticism is that it's OVER-promoted, not under-used. D ('public are getting a false picture of what forensics is really like') is one element from the first sentence (the CSI mention) but isn't the MAIN focus — the author moves past public perception in sentence 2 onward.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is B. The main focus of paragraphs 1–2 is that forensic practitioners overstate what their techniques can prove — they don't recognise the limits.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to read 'forensic science has to transform itself' and 'a change is already under way' as evidence that the SCIENCE has been extended — progress narrative.
distractors.why_wrong: The 'change' is the GOAL the author calls for, not the current state. Paragraphs 1–2 critique the state of forensics, they don't celebrate progress. A inverts the polarity of the author's claim. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the criticism front and centre.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'transform itself' and conclude that something is going wrong — maybe forensic evidence is being dismissed when it shouldn't be.
distractors.why_wrong: The author's complaint is OVER-claiming, not under-use: experts 'exaggerate what forensic techniques can tell us'. 'Played down' is the wrong direction. Step 5's option walk catches the polarity flip.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: the CSI opener IS about public perception, so 'public getting a false picture' feels like a fair summary of paragraph 1.
distractors.why_wrong: The CSI sentence sets up the article, but the MAIN focus shifts immediately to the practitioners themselves ('expert witnesses have tended to exaggerate'). The article is about the practitioners' overclaiming, not just public misperception. Step 2's paragraph-2 quote pins the focus on the practitioners.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-036.technique: On 'main focus of the first two paragraphs' items, find the THESIS sentence — usually the one starting with 'The problem is…' or 'In reality…'. The thesis is rarely the opening CSI/anecdote sentence; it's the second or third sentence that names the actual critique.
host-2015-verb2-ELF-036.pitfall: Opinion-piece openers often use a pop-culture hook (CSI here) that distracts you from the real claim. Trust the sentence with the explicit complaint marker ('The problem is…', 'has to transform itself') as the focus, not the hook.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-037.solution_path: The text says 'Expert witnesses command great authority in the eyes of jurors and judges, and problems arise when the so-called experts claim greater precision for their techniques than they can support.' Courts trust expert witnesses too much — that is D.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is IMPLIED about courts in relation to forensic evidence. So you want the takeaway the author is steering you toward — the unstated criticism behind the stated facts. Scan for sentences where the courts are the subject and a problem is named.

steps.title: Locate the implication
steps.text: Two related sentences. 'The big obstacle is the courts. Expert witnesses command great authority in the eyes of jurors and judges, and problems arise when the so-called experts claim greater precision for their techniques than they can support.' Also: 'In the US, some courts have adopted screening mechanisms to try to prevent untrustworthy experts from testifying, but this is a problem when judges without extensive scientific training have to decide whether practitioners know what they are talking about.' And: 'Courts have traditionally deferred to the scientific community to help them distinguish respected practitioners from charlatans.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: courts are the obstacle because they trust expert witnesses too much. Jurors and judges hand great authority to so-called experts, and problems arise when those experts overclaim. Judges aren't scientifically trained enough to push back. So the implied charge: courts put too much trust in people who claim forensic expertise.

steps.title: Vocabulary check
steps.text: 'Command great authority' = have a lot of power / be taken very seriously. 'Claim greater precision than they can support' = overstate what their methods can prove. 'Defer to' = let someone else decide. 'Shaky reputation' (in option B) = unreliable standing. 'Outdated research' (in option C) = old research that's been superseded.

steps.title: Match against the options
steps.text: Walk each option. A ('Their scientific competence is now considerably greater than it used to be') contradicts 'judges without extensive scientific training' — the article says judges lack the science background. B ('They are fully aware of the shaky reputation of forensic experts in general') contradicts 'Expert witnesses command great authority in the eyes of jurors and judges' — courts AREN'T aware; they trust the experts. C ('Their scientific ignorance makes them rely too much on outdated research') is close — the article does say judges lack scientific training — but the criticism isn't 'outdated research', it's 'over-trusting individual experts'. D ('They tend to put too much trust in witnesses claiming expertise in forensics') maps directly onto 'command great authority in the eyes of jurors and judges' + 'problems arise when the so-called experts claim greater precision than they can support'.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.text: The answer is D. The article repeatedly criticises courts for giving expert witnesses too much weight — D names that pattern in plain language.

distractors.why_tempting: It's tempting to assume modern courts must have improved their science literacy — and 'considerably greater than it used to be' fits a progress narrative.
distractors.why_wrong: The article says the opposite: 'judges without extensive scientific training have to decide whether practitioners know what they are talking about'. The science gap is current, not historical. Step 2's quoted material flatly contradicts A.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'screening mechanisms to try to prevent untrustworthy experts' and conclude that courts ARE aware of the problem and acting on it.
distractors.why_wrong: The screening mechanisms are a partial response — but the article says it's a problem because judges can't actually evaluate the experts. Courts are NOT 'fully aware of the shaky reputation'; they keep deferring to the experts. Step 3's paraphrase keeps the courts on the side of over-trust.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct: 'scientific ignorance' fits 'judges without extensive scientific training' — that pairing feels natural.
distractors.why_wrong: C names the wrong consequence of the ignorance — 'outdated research'. The article's complaint is that judges rely too much on EXPERTS (current ones), not on outdated research. Step 5's option walk catches the misplaced object of the over-reliance.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-037.technique: On 'implied about courts' items, find the sentence that names a SPECIFIC actor and a specific failure — here, 'Expert witnesses command great authority in the eyes of jurors and judges'. The correct option restates that specific actor-failure pair without adding a new ingredient (like 'outdated research').
host-2015-verb2-ELF-037.pitfall: Distractors often graft the right CAUSE (scientific ignorance) onto the wrong OBJECT (outdated research vs. over-trusted experts). Always check that the option matches the FULL complaint, not just one half of it.

host-2015-verb2-ELF-038.solution_path: The Massachusetts case shows fingerprint examiners insist their method is fail-safe while scientists say 'the reliability of fingerprinting will never be known until its accuracy is measured — something that has yet to be done'. So nobody can yet say for sure how reliable fingerprint evidence really is. That is A.

steps.title: Understand the question
steps.text: The prompt asks what is SAID about the use of fingerprint evidence in court. So you want what the article actually states — find the section about fingerprints in court and pull the most direct claim.

steps.title: Locate the fingerprint section
steps.text: The Massachusetts case sets it up. 'The point at issue was the reliability of fingerprint identification. The state argued that, since all fingerprint examiners believe fingerprint examination is fail-safe, the court should back it. The defendant argued that a broad cross-section of scientists and scholars who had studied the issue agreed that the reliability of fingerprinting will never be known until its accuracy is measured — something that has yet to be done.' Plus later: 'A century of fingerprint practice went by before the first attempt to measure its accuracy was published.'

steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English
steps.text: Boiled down: nobody has actually measured how reliable fingerprint identification is.
